title: Efficiency & market failure
summary:
## The big idea A2 micro begins by sharpening the question that runs underneath everything: *is this market doing a good job?* 'Good job' gets a precise meaning — **efficiency** — and 'bad job' gets one too — **market failure**. This chapter is the map for Units 6–8: every diagram and policy that follows is about markets falling short of efficiency and governments trying (and sometimes failing) to fix it. ## The four efficiencies - **Productive efficiency** — output is produced at the **lowest possible average cost**: no wasted resources. For the whole economy, that means being **on the PPC**; for a firm, producing at the **bottom of its average cost curve**. - **Allocative efficiency** — resources produce the *mix* of goods society most values. The precise condition: **price = marginal cost (P = MC)**. Price measures what the last unit is worth to consumers; MC measures what it cost society to make. If P > MC, society wants more; if P < MC, we've made too much. At P = MC, total surplus (consumer + producer) is maximised. - **Dynamic efficiency** — efficiency **over time**: innovation, investment and technology cutting costs and creating better products. Unlike the first two (static, at a point in time), dynamic efficiency needs *profits* to fund R&D — which creates a famous tension: the competitive markets that deliver static efficiency may generate too little profit for dynamic efficiency. - **X-inefficiency** — a firm's costs are simply **higher than they need to be**: slack management, overstaffing, waste. It thrives where competition is weak — the monopolist 'enjoying a quiet life'. The grand benchmark is **Pareto efficiency**: an allocation where **nobody can be made better off without making someone else worse off**. A perfectly competitive economy (under strict assumptions) reaches it; market failure means falling short.
**What each part means:** the yellow curve is the economy's **PPC** — the maximum combinations of two goods it can produce. Any point **on** the curve, like **A**, is **productively efficient**: no resources idle, no waste — making more of one good must mean less of the other (that's the Pareto idea in picture form). Point **B**, inside the curve, is **inefficient** — unemployment or X-inefficiency; more of *both* goods is possible. Point **C** is beyond today's capacity. Note: *every* point on the curve is productively efficient, but only **one** of them matches what society most wants — that one is also **allocatively** efficient.
## Market failure **Market failure** is when the free market **fails to allocate resources efficiently**, so society's welfare is not maximised — the core justification for government intervention. Distinguish the two degrees: - **Complete market failure** — the market is **missing** entirely: nobody supplies the good at all. Pure **public goods** (defence, street lighting) are the classic case — non-excludability creates the free-rider problem, so no private firm can charge. - **Partial market failure** — the market exists but delivers the **wrong quantity**: too much (demerit goods, pollution) or too little (merit goods, training). ## The sources of market failure — the A2 checklist 1. **Externalities** — spillover costs/benefits to third parties (next chapter's diagrams). 2. **Public goods** — non-excludable + non-rival → free riders → under- or non-provision. 3. **Merit and demerit goods** — mis-valued because of **information failure** (people underestimate education's benefits, smoking's harms). 4. **Information failure / asymmetric information** — one side knows more (lemons, insurance; next chapter). 5. **Monopoly / market power** — output restricted, **P > MC** → allocative inefficiency, plus X-inefficiency risk (Unit 7). 6. **Factor immobility** — labour and capital stuck in declining industries/regions → structural unemployment. 7. **Inequity** — the market's income distribution may be judged unfair (careful: a *normative* failure — Unit 8). ## Why it matters These definitions are the **yardstick for every evaluation** you'll write at A2: perfect competition is praised for reaching P = MC; monopoly is condemned for missing it, then partially forgiven for dynamic efficiency; every intervention is judged by whether it moves output toward the social optimum at acceptable cost. Learn the conditions verbatim — examiners reward precision.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Productive efficiency**: lowest AC / on the PPC. **Allocative**: **P = MC**, surplus maximised. - **Dynamic**: innovation over time (needs profit). **X-inefficiency**: costs above minimum (weak competition). - **Pareto efficiency**: no one can gain without someone losing. - **Market failure** = inefficient allocation. **Complete** = missing market (public goods); **partial** = wrong quantity (externalities, merit/demerit goods). - Checklist of causes: externalities, public goods, merit/demerit, information failure, monopoly (P > MC), factor immobility, inequity. - This chapter = the yardstick; Units 6–8 = the failures and fixes.

keyTerms.definition: Producing at the lowest possible average cost, using resources without waste.
Producing the mix of goods society most wants, where price equals marginal cost.
Improvements in efficiency over time through innovation and investment.
When a firm produces at a higher cost than necessary, often due to weak competition.
An allocation where no one can be made better off without making someone worse off.
When free markets fail to allocate resources efficiently, reducing social welfare.
Complete: the good is not provided at all; partial: it is provided in the wrong quantity.

diagrams: **Allocative efficiency:** on a demand/supply or cost/revenue diagram, mark the output where **P = MC** and show consumer + producer surplus maximised.
**Productive efficiency:** the lowest point of the average cost curve, or a point on the PPC.
Reference the **externality diagrams** (next chapter) to show welfare loss from partial market failure.
tips: Define **allocative efficiency as P = MC** and productive efficiency as **minimum AC** — precise conditions score marks.
Use **Pareto optimality** to explain what an efficient outcome means and why market failure falls short.
Classify failures as **complete (missing market)** vs **partial (wrong quantity)** — a neat framing.
Mention **dynamic efficiency** and **X-inefficiency** to show A2-level depth beyond static efficiency.
Treat this chapter as your **map** of Units 6–8; reference the specific failure diagrams when answering.

revisionNotes:
# Efficiency & market failure — quick revision

**Efficiency conditions (precise definitions score)**
- **Productive**: minimum average cost; economy on its PPC.
- **Allocative**: **P = MC**; the goods mix society wants; total surplus maximised.
- **Dynamic**: falling costs/better products over time via innovation & investment (needs profit).
- **X-inefficiency**: costs above the necessary minimum — a weak-competition disease.
- **Pareto optimal**: no one better off without another worse off.

**Market failure**
- Free market misallocates resources → welfare below maximum.
- **Complete** failure = missing market (pure public goods, free-rider problem).
- **Partial** failure = wrong quantity (over: demerit/pollution; under: merit/training).

**Causes checklist**
1. Externalities 2. Public goods 3. Merit/demerit goods 4. Information failure/asymmetry 5. Monopoly power (P > MC) 6. Factor immobility 7. Inequity (normative).

**Evaluation links**
- Perfect competition → static efficiency but maybe not dynamic.
- Monopoly → allocative + X-inefficiency, but possible dynamic gains.
- Every policy in Units 6–8 is judged against these yardsticks + government failure.
questions:
# Practice questions — Efficiency & market failure **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Allocative efficiency is achieved when: - A — firms produce at the lowest point of the average cost curve - B — price equals marginal cost - C — firms make only normal profit - D — output is inside the production possibility curve
▸ Show answer

B. P = MC means the value consumers place on the last unit equals its cost to society. A is productive efficiency; C concerns profit levels; D is inefficiency.

**2.** An economy operating at a point inside its PPC is: - A — productively inefficient - B — allocatively efficient - C — Pareto efficient - D — dynamically efficient
▸ Show answer

A. Inside the PPC, more of both goods could be produced — resources are idle or wasted, so production is inefficient (and the point cannot be Pareto optimal).

**3.** Street lighting is not supplied by private firms because non-payers cannot be excluded. This is an example of: - A — partial market failure - B — a demerit good - C — complete market failure (a missing market) - D — government failure
▸ Show answer

C. The free-rider problem means no private supplier can charge, so the market is missing entirely — complete market failure, the signature of a pure public good.

**4.** A monopolist with no competitors allows its production costs to drift well above the attainable minimum. This is best described as: - A — allocative inefficiency - B — X-inefficiency - C — dynamic efficiency - D — Pareto efficiency
▸ Show answer

B. Costs above the necessary minimum due to slack — the classic X-inefficiency of firms sheltered from competition. (The monopolist is likely also allocatively inefficient, but the cost drift itself is X-inefficiency.)

## Paper 2 / 4 style — data response > A state-owned electricity monopoly charges prices well above marginal cost, employs 30% more staff per customer than comparable utilities abroad, and has not upgraded its generation technology in twenty years. Blackouts are frequent. The government is considering breaking it up and letting private firms compete, but unions warn of job losses and campaigners fear higher prices for the poor. **(a)** Define 'X-inefficiency'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain **two** types of inefficiency, apart from X-inefficiency, shown by this monopoly. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how the introduction of competition might improve each type of inefficiency identified. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether breaking up the monopoly would necessarily increase economic welfare. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Producing at higher cost than necessary — organisational slack — typically where competition is absent. [1+1]

(b) Allocative inefficiency: P > MC (prices well above marginal cost) → too little consumed relative to the optimum. Dynamic inefficiency: no innovation/investment for twenty years. (Productive inefficiency also creditable via overstaffing.) [2+2]

(c) Competition → prices pushed toward MC (allocative); rivals force cost-cutting and staffing discipline (productive/X); the threat of losing customers spurs technology upgrades (dynamic). Develop each chain. [6]

(d) For: efficiency gains above. Against: electricity may be a natural monopoly (duplication wasteful); private firms may cut unprofitable rural supply; equity concerns for poor consumers; transition costs and job losses; regulation could achieve some gains without break-up. Judgement: welfare rise not guaranteed — depends on industry structure and the regulatory regime. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the difference between productive, allocative and dynamic efficiency. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether perfectly competitive markets achieve all three types of efficiency. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Minimum AC / on PPC; P = MC with surplus maximised; innovation over time. Precise conditions plus an example each.

(b) For: long-run equilibrium delivers P = MC and minimum AC (both static efficiencies). Against: only normal profit → little funding or incentive for R&D → dynamic efficiency doubtful (contrast Schumpeter's defence of monopoly profits); also assumes no externalities. Judgement: strong on static, weak on dynamic.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain what is meant by market failure and distinguish between its complete and partial forms. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss which single cause of market failure is the most damaging to an economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Inefficient allocation → welfare loss; complete = missing market (public goods/free riders); partial = wrong quantity (externalities, merit/demerit goods) — with examples.

(b) Candidates: externalities (climate change — vast, global, intergenerational); public goods (missing markets entirely); monopoly (P > MC but bounded); information failure (underlies merit/demerit). A strong answer picks one (climate-scale externalities are a defensible choice), argues magnitude/persistence/correctability, and acknowledges context-dependence.
